coredata.dc:title: Deep learning-enabled nanophotonic test leveraging poly-L lysine-tethered carbon quantum dots to assess the risk of cardiovascular disease

coredata.dc:description: The circulating cell-free mitochondrial DNA (ccf-mtDNA), in conjunction with circulating exosomes and NT-proBNP, represents a collective set of biomarkers crucial for the early identification and assessment of cardiovascular diseases (CVDs). In this context, our study explores an innovative quadruple-hybrid nano-photonic assay system engineered to identify all three distinct biomarkers. This cutting-edge assay comprises four sensing components: aminopyrene tethered carbon nanodots (CNDs) to efficiently detect circulating nucleic acids (ccf-NAs), followed by identification of ccf-mtDNA through PLL functionalized CNDs, and finally, targeting of NT-proBNP and circulating exosomes by graphene nanodots (GNDs-PLL) tethered with specific antibodies. The quadruple assay system demonstrated strong feasibility, high selectivity, and adaptability across various analytical platforms, with a detection limit of 0.369 fg and a quantification limit of 1.232 fg for ccf-mtDNA. The developed test overcomes the need for amplification and minimizes the detection time and assay-related complexities without disturbing the stability. The long short-term memory (LSTM) deep learning model accurately identified CVD and non-CVD samples with an overall accuracy rate of 99.86 % and validation loss of 0.25 % utilizing the sensor data and preliminary diagnostic data. The ability of the deep learning models to accurately discern CVD status suggests that integration of deep learning-based AI with biosensors further improves the precise diagnosis of CVD. Altogether, our observations highlight the promising potential of the developed AI-integrated biosensing system as a practical tool for comprehensive CVD risk assessment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The figure showing the surface functionalization of fluorescent nanomaterials (CNDs and GNDs) using 1-AP and PLL through EDC-NHS coupling chemistry. Target-specific substrates, such as a Probe for mtDNA D-loop, CD63 Ab for exosomes, and NT-proBNP Ab for proteins, are then added to enable selective capture and detection of analytes, i.e., NAs, ccf-mtDNA, circulating exosomes, and NT-proBNP. A quadruple nano photonic conjugate is used in this process, with PI as an intercalating dye for detection and a secondary antibody to confirm the binding of target molecules to the nanohybrid.

The figure illustrating the conjugation process of CND-PLL with the Probe using EDC-NHS. (A) Shows initial energies: −1260.16 for CND-PLL and −542.60 for G-NH2, with the conjugated product (CND-PLL-Probe) at −1675.27. (B) Displays 3D structures of CND-PLL, G-NH2, and the final CND-PLL-Probe, highlighting increased transition electron density on the CND surface and in the conjugated product.

The figure shows fluorescence microscopy images of various nanostructures and their interactions. (A) displays CND-1AP (blue) capturing NAs, with colocalization with PI (red) confirming nucleic acid binding. (B) shows CND-PLL (blue) binding ccf-mtDNA, confirmed by colocalization with PI (red). (C) illustrates GND-450 (blue) detecting exosomes, validated by red fluorescence from a secondary antibody. Finally, (D) presents GND-525 (green) capturing NT-proBNP, confirmed by colocalization with the red secondary antibody. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The figure presents the application of nanoconjugates for detecting biomarkers. In (A), CND-1AP is shown to be more effective than unconjugated CND in screening circulating NAs. In (B), CND-PLL-Probe demonstrates superior precision in detecting ccf-mtDNA compared to other controls. Graphs (C) and (D) highlight the sensitivity and specificity of CND-PLL-Probe for ccf-mtDNA detection, with (C) showing detection sensitivity from 1000 ng to 1 fg and (D) confirming specificity for the F1 mtDNA fragment. In (E), the nanoconjugate GND525-PLL-NT-proBNP efficiently identifies NT-proBNP in plasma, with specificity validated against controls.

The figure contains four graphs analyzing machine learning model performance. Graphs (A) and (B) are scatter plots showing strong linear relationships between actual values and predictions. Graph (C) tracks the LSTM model's loss, with both training and validation loss decreasing, indicating convergence. Graph (D) shows improving accuracy over epochs despite some fluctuations.

The figure illustrates the workflow of a fluorescent-based quadruple assay designed for the screening and detection of NAs, ccf-mtDNA, circulating exosomes, and NT-proBNP in circulation. This assay utilizes PLL-tethered CND (carbon nanodots), GND450, and GND525 (CND with AP specifically for all NAs) within a sample for efficient detection.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The circulating cell-free mitochondrial DNA (ccf-mtDNA), in conjunction with circulating exosomes and NT-proBNP, represents a collective set of biomarkers crucial for the early identification and assessment of cardiovascular diseases (CVDs). In this context, our study explores an innovative quadruple-hybrid nano-photonic assay system engineered to identify all three distinct biomarkers. This cutting-edge assay comprises four sensing components: aminopyrene tethered carbon nanodots (CNDs) to efficiently detect circulating nucleic acids (ccf-NAs), followed by identification of ccf-mtDNA through PLL functionalized CNDs, and finally, targeting of NT-proBNP and circulating exosomes by graphene nanodots (GNDs-PLL) tethered with specific antibodies. The quadruple assay system demonstrated strong feasibility, high selectivity, and adaptability across various analytical platforms, with a detection limit of 0.369 fg and a quantification limit of 1.232 fg for ccf-mtDNA. The developed test overcomes the need for amplification and minimizes the detection time and assay-related complexities without disturbing the stability. The long short-term memory (LSTM) deep learning model accurately identified CVD and non-CVD samples with an overall accuracy rate of 99.86 % and validation loss of 0.25 % utilizing the sensor data and preliminary diagnostic data. The ability of the deep learning models to accurately discern CVD status suggests that integration of deep learning-based AI with biosensors further improves the precise diagnosis of CVD. Altogether, our observations highlight the promising potential of the developed AI-integrated biosensing system as a practical tool for comprehensive CVD risk assessment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past two decades, there has been a remarkable surge in optical sensing research. This growth can be attributed to technological advancements in optics, photonics, and material science. Photoluminescence-based methods for developing optical sensors gained more attention due to their high sensitivity and selectivity in disease diagnosis [1]. Nanomaterial that emits fluorescence contains awide class of quantum dots (organic and inorganic). Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanodots (CNDs) [2] and graphene nanodots (GNDs) [3,4], have garnered significant attention in recent years. These nanomaterials exhibit unique optical properties, making them ideal candidates for various biological sensing applications [5–7]. The CNDs address the limitations of conventional organic dyes and nanomaterials and outperform traditional metallic quantum dots (QDs) [8]. It provides a range of advantages, including low molecular weight, minimal toxicity, simple surface customization, easily available raw material sources, cost-effective, minute particle size, exceptional fluorescence stability, customizable emission wavelengths, and robust biocompatibility [9]. CNDs and GNDs are characterized by a carbogenic core surrounded by stacked sp2-hybridized aromatic structures [10].

Conjugation of the CNDs and GNDs involves altering their surface chemistry using distinct molecules or functional groups and gaining the ability to finely adjust CNDs affinity for biological targets [11]. Poly-L-lysine (PLL) is a synthetic compound comprising several units of the amino acid L-lysine and can offer surface modification of nanomaterials for biosensing purposes. These units link via the amino group of one lysine molecule to the carboxyl group of another, resulting in the formation of a chain-like structure; PLL amino side chains have a pKa of roughly 9.0, making them positively charged under physiological circumstances [12]. Their functionality relies on electrostatic interactions, precisely between the positive charge of PLLs and the negative charge of CNDs. This unique property has spurred extensive research into employing PLLs as versatile agents, including nanocarriersandcoatings. PLL works by strengthening the electrostatic attraction. Applying to the surface creates an environment with more positively charged sites, allowing for significant and more stable nucleic acid (NA) molecule binding [13–15]. Additionally, 1-aminopyrene (1-AP), a pyrene derivative, is a linker for immobilizing NA onto carbon nanostructures, enabling π-π stacking interactions and intercalation with nucleobases. Combined with nanotechnological frameworks, these interactionshave paved the way for developing miniaturized sensing platforms for nucleic acid research, molecular diagnostics, and material science [16].

The major strength of biosensors is their capacity to transform biological interactions into a type of electric signal that can be detected and quantified. It is also crucial to focus on the effective analysis of minute variations throughout the biological processes when different biomolecules combine to diagnose diseases early. The use of biomarkers in CVD research and diagnosis has been a topic of significant interest. They can be categorized into traditional and newer biomarkers. Traditional biomarkers such as high-sensitivity C-reactive protein and cardiac troponins have been widely used. Still, their diagnostic and prognostic limitations have led to exploring newer biomarkers [17]. Biomarkers such asccf-mtDNA [18], circulating exosomes [19], and circulating NT-proBNPhave shown promise in enhancing the efficiency and accuracy of CVD diagnosis and risk assessment [20]. Circulating danger-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), including ccf-mtDNA, have emerged as valuable biomarkers for early disease detection and prognosis. These DAMPs comprise diverse substances encompassing lipids, sugars, metabolites, and nucleic acids released upon cellular damage. Its presence outside cells is associated with CVD onset and progression, influencing conditions like cardiac fibrosis, hypertrophy, and inflammation-linked diseases [21,22]. The release of ccf-mtDNA, triggered by oxidative stress and impaired autophagy, sparks inflammatory reactions in atherosclerosis. Exogenous mtDNA exacerbates endothelial dysfunction and activates receptors like TLR4 and TLR9, intensifying inflammation linked to myocarditis and dilated cardiomyopathy. After a heart attack, there is a surge in plasma mtDNA levels that correlates with inflammatory cytokines and impacts cardiac remodeling, leading to heart failure. Elevated plasma mtDNA levels align with heightened mortality rates in heart failure cases. Plasma-derived exosomes carrying mtDNA induce an inflammatory response via the TLR9-NFκB pathway. This cascade emphasizes the role of ccf-mtDNA in intercellular communication, notably influencing adverse cardiac remodeling and heart failure in CVD progression [21,23]. Moreover, circulating exosomes have been implicated in intercellular communication and the transport of various biomolecules in the cardiovascular system. They transport essential biomolecules, including DNA, RNA, miRNA, and proteins, between different types of cardiac cells [24]. Changes in the composition and content of exosomal cargo, especially under pathological conditions, signify underlying cardiac abnormalities, such as inflammation, oxidative stress, and impaired cellular processes. Monitoring these molecular changes within circulating exosomes may offer insights into the early stages of CVDs [25].

Furthermore, exosomes can be readily isolated from body fluids, allowing the characterization of both exosomes and their mediators, further enhancing their potential as non-invasive biomarkers for CVDs [26–29]. The study introduces innovative fluorescence-based nanoconjugates utilizing quadruple nanohybrid structures for the detection of theabove-mentioned biomarkers for CVD risk assessment. These nanoconjugates comprise aminopyrene-tethered CND (CND-1AP) acting as the capture component, effectively detecting ccf-NAs via π-π interactions. The second nanoconjugate system employs a GND450-PLL-CD63 antibody, specifically targeting exosomes. Additionally, our method integrates a system involving CND-PLL-Probe conjugates capturing components for ccf-mtDNA. Furthermore, our final component captures the NT-proBNP cardiac stress biomarker using the GND525-PLL-NT-proBNP antibody. This comprehensive approach demonstrates practicality and versatility in assessing cardiovascular risk. Various characterization methods have been used to analyze prepared nanoconjugates, including zeta potential, IR spectroscopy, and particle size analyzer, along with a novel approach for surface energy calculation using insilico methods. The performance ability was carried out using fluorescence microscopy, fluorometry, and flow cytometry.
The Regents and solvents acquired for this study were of thehighest available grade. Please refer to the “Supplementary Information” section for detailed information.

The procedure adopted for the isolation of NAs and exosomes was described in the “Supplementary information” section. After isolation, statistics-based correlations were analyzed between ccf-mtDNA copy number and CVD biomarker NT-proBNP. (Please refer to the “Supplementary information” section for detailed information).

Before creating the screening facet of the assay, we prepared a 1 mM solution of CNDs in water (1000 μl) and a 1 mM solution of 1-AP in ethanol (1000 μl). These solutions were combined and vortexed for 90 min at RT. Subsequently, the mixture was kept for overnight incubation at 4 °C. Following this incubation, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000×g for 10 min, and the resulting supernatant was stored at 4 °C [30] (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GND-PLL and CND-PLL conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: A combination of 50 μl of CNDs from the native dilution, 5 μl of EDC (1 mM), and NHS (1 mM) in the ratio of 1:1000:1000 was added in a separate Eppendorf to activate the surface functional groups, followed by vortexing for 20 min. Then, 50 μl of poly-L-lysine solution (0.01 %) was added to achieve a 1:1 ratio with CNDs. The required volume of 1X PBS was added to get the solution of 250 μl and vortexed for another 2 h. The mixture was then left for overnight incubation, and the prepared CND-PLL conjugates were appropriately evaluated. Similarly, GND-PLL was prepared by adding 50 μl of GNDs and 20 μl of PLL in a 1:1 ratio and surface activation using EDC/NHS coupling chemistry [30] (Fig. 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GND450-PLL-CD63 Ab, GND525-PLL-NT-proBNP Ab, and CND-PLL-Probe conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μl of CND-PLL was activated using 5 μl of EDC (1 mM) and NHS (1 mM) in the ratio of 1:1000:1000 in a separate Eppendorf tube followed by a 15-min incubation in ice-cold conditions. The mixture was added to 1 μl (100 pM) of 3′ end amine-modified Probe, achieving a 1:1000 ratio with CND concerning the number of particles. This was followed by a 30-min incubation period with intermittent vortexing. The mixture was then left to incubate overnight. Similarly, the CD63 and NT-proBNP antibody conjugation were prepared with GND450 and GND525 poly-L-Lysin conjugates, respectively. For this, 50 μl of prepared GND450-PLL reacted with 1 μl of CD63 antibody diluted in 1X PBS (1:2.5) and 50 μl of NT-proBNP from 1: 100-fold dilution of standard to get the ratio of 1:1000 ratio with 50 μl GND525-PLL for final conjugates [31] (Fig. 1). Characterization of prepared conjugates were evaluated through Zeta sizer and FTIR (Please refer to “Supplementary information” section for further information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Insilico energy calculations of CND-PLL conjugation and CND-PLL-Probe conjugation
ce:section.ce:para: The energy calculation process for CND-PLL conjugation and CND-PLL-Probe conjugation was done using Jaguar in Schrodinger Maestro. CND, PLL, and the Probe molecular structures were initially prepared and imported into Schrödinger Maestro. The CND-PLL conjugate was constructed by positioning or covalently linking the CND and PLL molecules. At the same time, the CND-PLL-Probe complex is formed by placing the probe molecule near the conjugate or linking it covalently. Geometry optimization is performed using Jaguar, employing specified convergence criteria and selecting an appropriate theory and basis set level. Energy calculations for the optimized structures are then executed using Jaguar, with careful selection of the calculation method and type. The resulting total energies are compared and analyzed to assess the stability and energetic changes upon conjugation, providing insights into the molecular interactions [32,33].

The developed quadruple assay’s ability to screen ccfNAs and specifically identify ccf-mtDNA along with circulating exosomes and NT-proBNP in plasma was assessed using fluorescence microscopy. Additionally, the evaluation involved quantification techniques such as fluorometry and flow cytometry to determine the assay’s precision (Fig. 1).

For CNDs conjugates, the microscopy images of plain native nanodots were taken. The next images include the conjugates with target analytes and intercalating dye. Coverslips were placed over the slides for analysis through fluorescence microscopy, employing appropriate filters for examination. Similarly, the samples GND450, GND450-PLL-CD63 Ab (with exosome), and secondary antibody, along with green GND525 and GND525-PLL-NT-proBNP Ab conjugates (with sample), were also examined using fluorescence microscopy [34].

Initially, 10 μl of plain CNDs and a CND-1AP solution in 1X PBS were added to the first two wells of the microplate, which served as the negative control. The wavelengths of 365 nm excitation and 450 nm emission were selected for measurement. Subsequently, a 10 μl sample of CND-1AP combined with 2 μl of ccf-NAs in 1X PBS was added to the next well, and fluorescence intensity was measured [35,39].

In the first well, 100 μl of NFW was taken for background reference. In the second well, a control mixture consisting of 90 µl NFW and 10 µl CNDs was added, and the third well contained 90 µl NFW, followed by 10 µl of poly-L-lysine-conjugated CNDs. The fourth contains 86 μl NFW, 10 μl CND-PLL with a Probe, and 2 μl of the PCR product with propidium iodide (PI). After 10–15 min incubation, fluorescence analysis at 365 nm excitation and 450 nm emission evaluated CND-PLL-Probe binding with ccf-mtDNA for PI 535 nm excitation and 615 nm emission wavelength selected [40].

A dilution range from 1000 ng to 1 fg of mtDNA was prepared. The first well with NFW served as a blank reference in the microplate setup. The second well was prepared with a mixture of 85.341 μl NFW, 10 μl CND-PLL-Probe, and 2.659 μl of the PCR product, followed by treatment with 2 μl PI. Other wells were similarly arranged with varying concentrations of mtDNA PCR product, from 1000 ng, 1 ng, 1 pg, and 1 fg, each treated with PI. Fluorescence analysis was measured at 365 nm excitation and 450 nm emission to analyze CND-PLL-Probe binding with ccf-mtDNA, while 535 nm excitation and 615 nm emission confirmed the probe and ccf-mtDNA conjugation through PI binding [35]. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) was calculated using the formula,

where, sensitivity (SEN) reflects the change in fluorescence signal per unit analyte concentration, indicating assay responsiveness. SEN−2 (sensitivity squared) normalizes measurement variability against assay sensitivity, showing how noise impacts detection at low concentrations. Var(x) is the variance of blank measurements, representing noise in fluorescence without analyte; higher blank variance raises the LOD. H0 captures baseline measurement variability based on calibration data, adjusting the LOD by accounting for a minimum detectable signal. Var(ycal) is the variance in calibration readings at known analyte concentrations, reflecting consistency in fluorescence measurements across concentrations [37,38].

In the microplate setup, the initial well was utilized as a reference blank, containing 100 μl of NFW. Subsequently, the second well was prepared with 86 μl of NFW, 10 μl of CND-PLL carrying a specific probe, and 2 μl of the PCR amplicon (fragment 1) with PI. In successive 8 wells, 8 mtDNA fragments were added with CND-PLL-Probe conjugate. After a 10–15-min incubation period, fluorescence intensity was measured. Fluorescence analysis employed 365 nm excitation and 450 nm emission wavelengths to evaluate CND-PLL-Probe binding with ccf-mtDNA [35].

To assess NT-proBNP capturing efficiency, a comparative fluorometric analysis was performed with three different sample groups. The first contained 90 μl of NFW and 10 μl of GND525s, the second had 90 μl of NFW followed by 10 μl of poly-L-lysine-conjugated GND525, the third consisted of 90 μl of NFW and 10 μl of GND525-PLL with an NT-proBNP antibody. The fourth, fifth, and sixth wells comprised 86 μl of NFW with 10 μl of GND525 nanoconjugate with 2 μl of the negative control (The negative control involved treating the sample with a proteinase K denaturing reagent to deactivate NT-proBNP) positive control 1, and positive control 2, respectively, along with 2 μl of a secondary antibody. Fluorescence is measured in fluorometry [42,43].

Lastly, flow cytometry analysis was employed to analyze the performance of the nanohybrid. The GND450-PLL-CD63 Ab nanoconjugate capturing exosome vesicles has been evaluated against native CD63 Ab. and GND525-PLL-NT-proBNP Ab nano conjugate capturing NT-proBNP evaluated against native NT-proBNP Ab. (Please refer to the “Supplementary information” section for further information) (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integrating AI for CVD risk assessment
ce:section.ce:para: The integration of AI in CVD risk assessment involves three distinct steps. The first step focused on using AI to predict the concentration of ccf-mtDNA and NT-proBNP in samples through multimode analysis of fluorescence intensity. Experimental datasets for ccf-mtDNA and NT-proBNP levels were acquired, and data analysis began by importing essential libraries like Pandas, NumPy, and Matplotlib into Jupyter Notebook. The Ridge regression model was trained and evaluated on two datasets to assess its predictive performance on molecular features, specifically DNA and BNP-related data, i.e., Dataset1 (“DNA.xlsx”) and Dataset2 (“BNP.xlsx”), respectively. Ridge regression was selected to handle potential multicollinearity in the data by imposing a penalty on large coefficients, which improves model stability and predictive accuracy. The method involved data preprocessing, including imputation for missing values and feature scaling to ensure compatibility with regression models. The datasets were split into training and testing subsets to validate model generalizability. After training, the model was saved using joblib, enabling easy deployment. When loaded, the saved Ridge regression model was employed to make predictions on the test data, producing estimates with reasonable Mean Squared Error (MSE) and R-squared values.

In the second step, data on CVD and non-CVD features were collected, pre-processed, and utilized to train an LSTM model. Initially, the dataset was loaded from an Excel file. The features (X), comprising Systolic pressure, Diastolic pressure, Weight, Smoking, Alcohol, proBNP (pg) Concentration, and ccf-mtDNA (ng) concentration, were separated from the target variable (y), labeled as 'Diagnosis' for CVD or non-CVD. Since the target variable was categorical, it was encoded using LabelEncoder, and the features were standardized using StandardScaler. The dataset was split into training and testing sets with an 80–20 ratio, where 80 % of the data was used for training and 20 % for testing. Specifically, within the training data, 20 % was set aside for validation during model training to monitor the model's performance on unseen data. The LSTM model was constructed using the Keras Sequential API. It consisted of one LSTM layer with 64 units, a Dense layer with 32 units, and a final output layer with a single unit using a sigmoid activation function to predict probabilities for binary classification. The input shape for the LSTM layer was defined as (X_train.shape [1], 1), reshaping the training and test datasets to three dimensions suitable for LSTM processing. The model was compiled using the Adam optimizer and binary cross-entropy as the loss function, and it was trained for 100 epochs with a batch size of 12. During training, the model's performance was evaluated using both training and validation datasets, and accuracy metrics were calculated. Additional evaluations included generating confusion matrices and ROC curves to comprehensively assess the model's predictive performance.

Finally, the models from step one (ccf-mtDNA_model and proBNP_model) were integrated to predict biomarker levels, which, along with other features, served as input for the LSTM model to predict CVD or non-CVD diagnosis [41]. (Please refer to the “Supplementary information” section for further information).

The analysis utilizing Pearson's product-moment correlation reveals a statistically significant positive relationship between ccf-mtDNA copy number and NT-proBNP. The calculated correlation coefficient of approximately 0.224 indicates a weak yet discernible positive linear association between the two variables, with the p-value of 0.035 further supporting the significance of the relationship. With a 95 % confidence interval spanning from 0.0168 to 0.4133, the true correlation coefficient likely falls within this range. These findings indicate the potential utility of ccf-mtDNA as a biomarker for CVD risk assessment, particularly when correlated with NTproBNP (CVD-specific biomarker) (Fig. S1).

Exploring the diagnostic potential of ccf-NAs, especially ccf-mtDNA, we developed a fluorescence-based quadruple-assay system for risk assessment of CVDs. The assay’s “capture and screening” component contains a nano-hybrid where 1-AP binds to CNDs via π-π forces, forming stable anchoring on the carbon surface through robust intra-molecular noncovalent interactions. This CND-1-AP configuration, with its fluorescence and π-based stacking properties, efficiently captures ccf-NAs without complex hybridization or amplification steps. The attachment of 1-AP to CND occurs at physiological pH (pH 7.4), ensuring optimal conditions for the formation of this potent quadruple-labeled nano-hybrid, leveraging the pyrenyl group’s configuration and nucleobases within the NA backbone structure. For detecting mtDNA, the CND is equipped with surface PLL, which links to an oligonucleotide probe that complements the long fragment of the mtDNA D-loop region (forming the CND-PLL-Probe conjugate). The coupling with the probe was achieved using EDC-NHS coupling chemistry. Similarly, GND450-PLL-CD63Ab and GND525-PLL-NT-proBNP Ab conjugation is achieved by EDC-NHS coupling Chemistry. These assay segments complete the identification and detection process within the system.

After the synthesis, the developed nanoconjugates were characterized, in which the Zeta sizer analysis revealed a marked alteration in the particle size distribution of CND and CND-PLL upon surface modification with Poly-L-Lysine (Fig. S2). Uncoated CNDs predominantly exhibited particles ranging from 1 to 10 nm, whereas CND-PLL exhibited a dominant population of particles exceeding 150 nm. This shift signifies a successful surface modification. Similarly, a noticeable shift in particle size distribution was observed in the case of GND; uncoated GND displayed a prevalent population at 200 nm, while GND-PLL, after surface modification, exhibited a dominant population of particles at 610 nm. Zeta potential measurements demonstrate that uncoated CNDs have a surface charge of −7.66 mV, contributing to their stability. Coating with PLL increases the negative charge to −10.6 mV for CND-PLL, significantly enhancing its electrostatic stability and reducing aggregation potential. This improved stability due to PLL coating holds promise for diverse applications requiring stable nanomaterials. Zeta potential measurements also indicate that uncoated GNDs have a charge of −5.74 mV after coating with PLL to form GND-PLL, and the negative charge is reduced to −3.51 mV (Fig. S2). This change might affect the electrostatic stability of GND-PLL and thus confirms the successful surface modification of CNDs and GNDs with PLL using EDC-NHS coupling chemistry.

The IR spectra of CNDs and CND-PLL share characteristics like OH stretching (3800–3000 cm−1) from hydrogen-bonded groups. CND exhibits peaks at 2826 cm−1 (CH stretching in aliphatic groups) and 2345 cm−1 (CN stretching). CND-PLL shows similar peaks at 2884 cm−1 (slightly shifted CH stretching) and 2386 cm−1 (CN stretching), plus a new one at 1513 cm−1 (NH bending in amide groups from PLL). Additionally, CND displays 1767 cm−1 (CO stretching in ketones/aldehydes) and 1634 cm−1 (CC stretching in aromatic rings), while CND-PLL mirrors the latter and adds 1075 cm−1 (CO stretching in esters) and 947 cm−1 (CH bending in aliphatic groups). Notably, CNDs display 653 cm−1 (CH bending in aromatics), which differs from CND-PLL, exhibiting a peak of 705 cm−1 (NH bending in amides). Also, GNDs display the 667.40 cm−1 peaks (CH bending), which differ from the 703 and 735 cm−1 peaks (NH bending) of GND-PLL (Fig. S3). These variations stem from the presence of ketone/aldehyde and PLL components in the CND-PLL spectrum and GND-PLL spectrum, distinguishing it from CNDs and GNDs, respectively, and thus verifying the surface functionalization of CNDs and GNDs.

Further for characterization and confirmation of surface activation and conjugation of CNDs via EDC-NHS chemistry Insilico energy calculations employed various formulas such as density functional theory (DFT) utilizing the B3LYP-D3 method, the energies provided represent the self-consistent field (SCF) energies of the molecules: Energy of CND-PLL: −1263.161489 Hartrees (SCF energy); Energy of G-NH2: −542.602314 Hartrees (SCF energy); Energy of CND-PLL-NH2-G: −1675.27210768763 Hartrees (SCF energy) (Fig. 2 ). These SCF energies are crucial as they encapsulate the total electronic energy of each molecule, factoring in the self-consistent field within the computational framework utilized for the calculations.
ΔE = Energy of Products − Energy of Reactants
ΔE = Energy of CND-PLL-NH2-G − (Energy of CND-PLL + Energy of G-NH2)
ΔE = −1675.27210768763 − (−1263.161489 – 542.602314)
ΔE = −1675.27210768763 + 1805.763803
ΔE ≈ −1675.27210768763 + 1805.763803
ΔE ≈ 130.491695 Hartrees

The notable observation centers around the disparity in total energies between the reactants CND-PLL + Guanine-NH2 (G-NH2) and the resulting product (CND-PLL-NH2-G) achieved through EDC-NHS coupling chemistry. This method is commonly employed in bioconjugation, especially within peptide or bioconjugate chemistry. EDC serves to activate carboxylic acids, facilitating amide bond formation. Meanwhile, NHS, when combined with EDC, forms an NHS ester known for its reactivity with primary amines. Upon the reaction of CND-PLL and G-NH2 with EDC and NHS, the activated carboxylic acid groups of CND-PLL can react with the primary amine groups of G-NH2. EDC catalyzes the formation of an amide bond between these groups, while NHS plays a role in stabilizing the intermediate compound. By calculating the energy difference, derived from subtracting the combined energies of the reactants from the product's energy (−1675.40415333689 − (−1263.161489) − (−542.602314)), it's evident that the formation of CND-PLL-NH2-G from CND-PLL and G-NH2 is energetically favored within the scope of these DFT calculations and SCF energy values. The calculated energy change for this transformation is approximately 130.491695 Hartrees, indicating a decrease in energy and suggesting an exothermic nature based on these computational predictions. This energy disparity indicates that the chemical transformation leading to CND-PLL-NH2-G likely results in a reduction in the overall energy state of the system or potentially releases energy [32] (Fig. 2A).

In addition to this, the eigenvalue of 0.92, derived from natural transition orbitals (NTOs) analysis after binding CND-PLL with the G-NH2 end of the probe, is associated with a specific excitation state, indicating the considerable intensity and contribution of this excitation to the electronic transition within the system. This eigenvalue measures the significance and weight attributed to the involved NTOs during this electronic transition, highlighting their pivotal role in defining alterations within the electronic structure from the ground state to an excited state. The observed value of 0.92 signifies a robust involvement of the associated NTOs, exerting a notable influence on the character and nature of the excitation state. This insight highlights the substantial impact of these specific orbitals in shaping the electronic transition, signifying their noteworthy contribution to the overall electronic structure of the system. The electron transition cloud of the product in (Fig. 2B) represents the amplification of the fluorescence property of the system [32,33].

After characterization, the developed nanoconjugates were screened for detection capability using fluorescence microscopy, fluorometry, and flow cytometry. The system efficiently captured both ccf-NAs through nucleobase self-assembly, driven by robust, attractive forces, and ccf-mtDNA via probe conjugation, specifically with the D-loop region of ccf-mtDNA. This successful capturing was confirmed using intercalating dye, showcasing the dual-color nano-composite image of both CND: ccf-NAs and ccf-mtDNA: CND-PLL-Probe-mtDNA in fluorescence microscopy images. Additionally, GND450-PLL-CD63Ab and GND525-PLL-NT-proBNP Ab conjugate bind to the exosome surface antigen and NT-proBNP protein, respectively, by antigen–antibody reaction. This capturing was confirmed using a secondary antibody (PE-CY 5.5 conjugate), composed of phycoerythrin, and emits red–orange fluorescence when excited by light. The dual-color composite structure of GND450-PLL-CD63 Ab: exosome and GND525-PLL-NT-proBNP Ab: NT-proBNP was successfully captured as fluorescence microscopy images (Fig. 3 ). A merged image was also captured that shows the overlapping of plain nanoconjugates with analytes. The last images of the graph explain the amount of overlapping.

Fluorometry was employed to assess alterations in fluorescence intensity after conjugation of CND-1AP and its interaction with target NAs. This method precisely quantifies changes in fluorescence emission resulting from binding events between CND-1AP and specific NA targets. Our fluorometry analysis revealed a significant increase in fluorescence intensity in CND-1AP upon binding with ccf-NAs, in contrast to unconjugated CND-1AP (Fig. 4 A). This finding supports the potential of the CND-1AP nanohybrid for ccf-NAs screening in plasma samples. These changes in fluorescence intensity likely originate from an enlarged delocalization region and a reduced bandgap due to π-based connections between 1-AP, CNDs, and interactions with nucleic acids [45,46].

The applicability of the CND-PLL nanoconjugate for detecting ccf-mtDNA was assessed using fluorometry. The decrease in fluorescence intensity of CNDs post-surface functionalization with PLL can be attributed to static quenching. The attachment of PLL molecules to the CND surface potentially leads to the formation of a complex that impedes the emissive capability of the CNDs. This complex formation induces changes in the local chemical environment surrounding the CNDs, hindering their effective light emission. These alterations likely disrupt fluorescence emission pathways or modulate energy transfer processes, decreasing fluorescence intensity [44]. An increase in fluorescence intensity was observed upon the specific binding of the CND-PLL-Probe conjugate to the ccf-mtDNA fragment. This augmentation could be attributed to a combination of reduced quenching effects, modifications in the local environment of the CNDs, and possible alterations in the proximity or interactions among the CNDs. These factors collectively contribute to an enhanced fluorescence signal. The interaction, facilitated by incorporating PI as an intercalating dye, delineates the bonds formed between the probe and the mtDNA fragment (Fig. 4B).

After the applicability assessment was confirmed, the sensitivity analysis was performed to estimate the nanohybrid system's detection limit for ccf-mtDNA. The technique demonstrated exceptional sensitivity, detecting ccf-mtDNA even at small concentrations (Fig. 4C). In various ranges of concentrations of mtDNA, the high sensitivity of the developed nanohybrid was found at 1000 ng and exponentially decreased with decreasing ccf-mtDNA concentration up to 1 fg. The LOD of this method for sensing ccf-mtDNA was sensitive up to 7.656 fg, and the limit of LOQ was approximately 23.2 fg [35].

The specificity of the CND-PLL-Probe conjugate was assessed against all 9 mtDNA fragments, revealing its successful and robust binding affinity specifically to the D-loop region of the ccf-mtDNA sequence within a complex biological matrix. The nanoconjugate shows more binding efficiency with fragment 1 compared to the remaining 8 fragments. This is due to the similarity of the designed probe sequence that matches the sequence of fragment 1. It underscores the nanoconjugate efficacy and targeted selectivity in molecular recognition [39] (Fig. 4D).

We comprehensively evaluated the GND525-PLL-NT-proBNP Ab conjugate's suitability for targeting the NT-proBNP CVD biomarker across our sample set. The assessment involved capturing and detecting NT-proBNP in enriched samples and comparing these results against a negative control. Additionally, we employed a secondary antibody to validate the capture and detection of NT-proBNP protein (Fig. 4E). The fluorescence intensity observed in positive controls 1 and 2 significantly exceeded that of the unbound GND525-PLL-NT-proBNP Ab conjugate at both wavelengths. This compellingly confirms the successful detection of NT-proBNP protein, further supported by the secondary antibody's validation of the capture process.

Next, the exosome detection and validation encompassed two freshly isolated exosome samples (A and B) utilizing GND450 and secondary Ab and performed in flow cytometry. The quadrant positioned at the bottom left represents a collection of biomarkers negative for both parameters (GND450 and secondary Ab). In contrast, the top left quadrant indicates biomarkers displaying positivity for secondary Ab binding. Moving to the bottom right quadrant, it illustrates biomarkers exhibiting positivity for exosome CND 450 binding. Lastly, the upper right quadrant signifies biomarkers simultaneously expressing both parameters. Our investigation unveiled significant fluorescence intensity shifts observed during the interaction of unstained exosomes with CD63 Ab at 1:100 and 1:200 ratio, alongside secondary Ab into the top left quadrant from the bottom left, which signifies the binding of exosomes with the antibodies. Notably, fluorescence spectral shifts towards the upper right quadrant were evident between the GND450-CD63Ab and secondary Ab interactions. This distinct population predominantly occupied the dual-positive quadrant, denoting concurrent GND450 positivity and exosome binding, as depicted in [31,40] (Fig. S4).

Similarly, through flow cytometry, we validated the GND525-NT-proBNPAb against NT-proBNP-rich samples. Negative controls treated with proteinase K were prepared to verify NT-proBNP presence. Our investigation unveiled a notable fluorescence intensity shift upon the binding of NT-proBNP to the GND525-NT-proBNPAb conjugates, along with secondary Ab. These observations underscore the potential utility of capturing and identifying functionalities of GND525-NT-proBNP Ab conjugate for specific target NT-proBNP [31,45] (Fig. S5).

The quadruple nanoconjugate-based approach exhibits promise in effectively screening circulating nucleic acids, such as mtDNA. This methodology leverages the specific interaction dynamics between GND450 and exosomes and GND525 and NT-proBNP. The results obtained from three analytical methods emphasize the quadruple assay's notable traits: high selectivity, adaptability, and capacity for real-time quantitative identification of ccf-mtDNA. The fluorescence-based quadruple assay provides insightful data on varying levels of ccf-mtDNA, exosomes, and NT-proBNP in both normal and diseased states. This information could significantly contribute to the analysis of altered biological processes, aiding in early risk assessment and prognosis of CVDs.

Moreover, AI has enhanced detection and diagnostic capability to a great extent. The assessment of the AI's predictive performance concerning ccf-mtDNA concentration from dataset 1 and NT-proBNP from dataset 2 yielded notably favorable outcomes. Regarding ccf-mtDNA concentration prediction, the ridge regression model demonstrated a remarkably minimal Mean Squared Error, estimated at approximately 1.7241, alongside achieving a pristine R-squared score of 0.999979 (Fig. 5 A). Analogously, in predicting NT-proBNP from dataset 2, the AI exhibited exceptional precision, characterized by an exceedingly low Mean Squared Error of approximately 2.69558 and an impeccable R-squared score of 0.9999945 (Fig. 5B). These findings underscore the AI's extraordinary proficiency in accurately capturing the associations between the independent predictors and the respective target variables in both datasets. The inconsequential errors and perfect R-squared scores substantiated the AI's capacity to align its predictions impeccably with the observed values, thereby emphasizing its efficacy and dependability in predictive modeling endeavors [46]. Understanding the performance and behavior of machine learning models is crucial for improving their predictive accuracy and generalization capabilities. The learning curve analysis demonstrates that the Ridge Regression model performs well and does not suffer from overfitting. The convergence of the training and validation errors and their decreasing trend indicate that the model effectively balances bias and variance, improving predictions (Fig. S6).

Subsequently, the outcome of the LSTM-DL model's diagnostic performance for CVD was highly promising; this level of accuracy, coupled with an overall accuracy rate of 99.86 % and validation loss of 0.25 %, highlights the model's robustness in accurately discerning CVD status based on the input data (Fig. 5C and D). The confusion matrix illustrates the model performance with 552 true negatives (TN) and 854 true positives (TP); the model effectively identifies individuals without and with CVD, respectively. Notably, the absence of false positives (FP) and the low count of false negatives (FN), totaling only 2, underscore the model's precision and reliability in its predictions. (Fig. S7A) The violin plots for Graph B reveal that the model maintains a balanced distribution of probabilities across different classes. This even spread is essential for ensuring the model performs consistently well across all classes without showing any significant biases or anomalies. Such a balanced distribution is crucial for the model’s overall performance and fairness. (Fig. S7B). An AUC value close to 1 in Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves for an LSTM model indicates excellent model performance. The Training and Validation ROC curves have an AUC of 1.00 and achieved perfect classification performance on both the training and validation datasets. This perfect AUC score suggests that the LSTM model is highly effective at distinguishing between classes without any errors during both the training and validation phases (Fig. S7C and D). The LSTM model's cross-validation performance, as depicted in a Mean Accuracy of 0.9879, suggests that the model's performance is relatively consistent across different cross-validation folds. This consistency indicates that the model has a stable performance and is less likely to be overly sensitive to the specific data used for training and testing. The high Mean Accuracy near 1.0 would signify strong predictive performance; a smaller standard deviation of ± 0.0241 indicates that the performance across different folds is tightly clustered around the mean, suggesting reliability in the model's predictions (Fig. S8). Such findings indicated the potential utility of the LSTM model as a valuable tool in clinical settings for assisting healthcare professionals in making accurate and timely CVD diagnoses [38].

We herein designed and developed a new fluorescence-based quadruple nanohybrid biosensor for risk assessment of CVDs. The assay utilizes a novel facet of functionalized carbon nanodots to capture four biomarkers simultaneously, which include ccf-NAs, ccf-mtDNA, exosomes, and NT-proBNP. The surface modification was carried out using EDC and NHS activation, allowing covalent bonding of probes and antibodies to the nanodot surfaces. Both CNDs and GNDs maintained their colloidal stability following surface modification, with no significant aggregation observed. This conjugation led to a slight increase in particle size due to the larger molecular weight of the antibodies and probe, but stability remained intact. The conjugated nanodots displayed good stability, though they were more sensitive to storage conditions like temperature, where fluctuations could potentially lead to antibody/probe degradation. DFT-D3 total energy calculations were integrated to validate the probe conjugation, demonstrating increased post-probe conjugation over the CND-PLL substrate. Meanwhile, the microscopy, fluorometry, and flow cytometry analysis confirmed the ability of the developed assay system to detect the biomarkers, as mentioned earlier, precisely. Our results highlighted exceptional selectivity, sensitivity, adaptability, and capacity for real-time quantification of biomarkers. Upon integration with the deep learning model, this detection technology showed exceptional predictive performance, with minimal errors and high accuracy (Fig. 6 ).

The LSTM-DL model shows a promising diagnostic accuracy of 99.86 % for CVD, effectively identifying individuals with high diagnostic probability and reliability. These results highlighted the potential utility of the AI and LSTM models in assisting healthcare professionals with accurate and timely CVD diagnoses. It should be noted that the developed quadruple nanoconjugate-based approach integrated with deep learning offers a simple, rapid, economically feasible, and minimally invasive mode of CVD risk assessment. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time a quadruple nanohybrid biosensor with LSTM model prediction has been assessed for the risk assessment of CVDs with the ccf-mtDNA and NTproBNP levels. The innovative quadruple-hybrid nanohybridization technology for CVD biomarker detection offers several significant advantages over traditional methods. It enhances sensitivity and specificity, allowing for the reliable identification of low-abundance biomarkers like ccf-mtDNA, NT-proBNP, etc, which surpasses the capabilities of conventional assays that typically require higher concentrations. Additionally, this system enables multiplexing, facilitating simultaneous detection of multiple biomarkers ccf-mtDNA, circulating exosomes, and NT-proBNP, thereby providing a more comprehensive assessment of cardiovascular health and allowing for quicker risk assessment. The assay also reduces complexity and overall detection time by minimizing the need for amplification steps common in traditional methods, which streamlines workflows in clinical settings. Furthermore, the integration of deep learning models into the analysis significantly enhances diagnostic accuracy in distinguishing between CVD and non-CVD samples. After appropriate validations, the method may prove beneficial for large-scale population-based screening of CVDs.
